feed.title: Combined summary - Proposal discussion: BIP39 native-language display wordlists

entry.title: Combined summary - Proposal discussion: BIP39 native-language display wordlists
entry.summary: Daniel Osemberg has proposed a new idea regarding the BIP39 recovery phrases, suggesting an enhancement to support additional native languages in the display/input layer without altering the underlying cryptographic process. The proposal maintains that the canonical mnemonic should remain the standard English BIP39 wordlist, with PBKDF2 applied on the canonical English form. Native-language lists would be index-paired with the English BIP39 wordlist, meaning each native word corresponds directly to the same 0-2047 index as its English counterpart. This approach allows wallets to display or accept recovery phrases in native languages for user experience (UX) purposes, while internally normalizing back to the canonical English mnemonic before seed generation. The motivation behind this proposal stems from the challenges non-English speakers face when using English recovery words to back up and restore Bitcoin wallets. These challenges include increased risk of UX issues, spelling mistakes, misunderstandings, and reduced confidence during the backup and recovery processes. By introducing native language support, the proposal aims to enhance multilingual recovery UX while ensuring compatibility with existing BIP39 behaviors. It is crucial to note that this initiative does not introduce a new seed scheme, replace BIP39, establish a new cryptographic standard, alter PBKDF2 input, or propose a wallet-specific format. Instead, it serves as a display/input convention for wallets that wish to support native-language recovery UX while preserving canonical BIP39 compatibility. The draft implementation and wordlists can be accessed [here](https://github.com/osem23/bip39-wordlists-tzur). Feedback is sought on several aspects of the proposal, including its suitability for the BIP process, whether it should be considered informational rather than standards-track, potential hidden risks associated with the index-paired approach, practical value for wallet developers, handling of native-speaker review and normalization rules, and any prior work that might influence further development of this idea. In response to feedback, Daniel clarifies that the proposal involves reinterpretation of existing BIP39 wordlists into separate, index-parallel display wordlists. These are intended solely for rendering and accepting user-facing mnemonics in another language while keeping the canonical English BIP39 mnemonic as the seed of record. He emphasizes that localized words would never be directly passed into PBKDF2; instead, the process consistently reverts to the canonical English mnemonic for critical operations like checksum validation and key derivation. To prevent ambiguity, especially in cases where legacy non-English BIP39 and proposed display lists coexist, Daniel suggests explicit mode selection and maintaining metadata such as language code and wordlist version to ensure clarity during restoration. Hence, the proposal prioritizes clear differentiation and explicit mode indication to safeguard against potential confusion and fund loss.
